grading.extracted_answer: To reconstruct the sequence of sibling-merge operations, follow this algorithmic approach: 1. Data Representation: Represent the initial tree using adjacency lists where each node stores its children in their original order. Represent the final tree similarly. 2. Mapping Construction: Perform a simultaneous post-order traversal (or a DFS) on both the initial and final trees. For each node u in the initial tree and its corresponding node v in the final tree, create a mapping between their children. Specifically, map the i-th child of u in the initial tree to the j-th child of v in the final tree. Since the problem guarantees a valid transformation, the children of u that are merged into a single child of v will appear as a contiguous segment in the initial child list of u. 3. Merge Identification: For every node u in the initial tree, examine its children list. Compare this list to the list of children of its corresponding node v in the final tree. If node v has a child that represents a merged group of children from u, identify the range of indices [i, j] in u's original children list that correspond to this single child in v. For each such segment [i, j] where j > i, generate merge operations. To merge children ci, ci+1, ..., cj into one, output the operations (ci, ci+1), (ci+1, ci+2), ..., (cj-1, cj) (or any sequence that reduces the segment to one node). 4. Ordering of Operations: To ensure the operations are valid (i.e., we don't attempt to merge nodes that have already been merged or are part of a subtree being moved), perform the merges in post-order traversal of the initial tree. This ensures that all necessary merges within a subtree are completed before the parent node is processed. 5. Implementation Detail: Use a Disjoint Set Union (DSU) or a simple array to track the current 'representative' ID of a node after merges to ensure the output uses the correct current identifiers.

Submitted final result text:
FINAL_ANSWER: To reconstruct the sequence of sibling-merge operations, follow this algorithmic approach: 1. Data Representation: Represent the initial tree using adjacency lists where each node stores its children in their original order. Represent the final tree similarly. 2. Mapping Construction: Perform a simultaneous post-order traversal (or a DFS) on both the initial and final trees. For each node u in the initial tree and its corresponding node v in the final tree, create a mapping between their children. Specifically, map the i-th child of u in the initial tree to the j-th child of v in the final tree. Since the problem guarantees a valid transformation, the children of u that are merged into a single child of v will appear as a contiguous segment in the initial child list of u. 3. Merge Identification: For every node u in the initial tree, examine its children list. Compare this list to the list of children of its corresponding node v in the final tree. If node v has a child that represents a merged group of children from u, identify the range of indices [i, j] in u's original children list that correspond to this single child in v. For each such segment [i, j] where j > i, generate merge operations. To merge children ci, ci+1, ..., cj into one, output the operations (ci, ci+1), (ci+1, ci+2), ..., (cj-1, cj) (or any sequence that reduces the segment to one node). 4. Ordering of Operations: To ensure the operations are valid (i.e., we don't attempt to merge nodes that have already been merged or are part of a subtree being moved), perform the merges in post-order traversal of the initial tree. This ensures that all necessary merges within a subtree are completed before the parent node is processed. 5. Implementation Detail: Use a Disjoint Set Union (DSU) or a simple array to track the current 'representative' ID of a node after merges to ensure the output uses the correct current identifiers. JUSTIFICATION: This approach correctly identifies the contiguous segments of siblings that were merged by comparing the child-lists of corresponding nodes in the initial and final trees, and uses post-order traversal to ensure a valid sequence of operations.
